key_points: The Atmosphere's thermal structure is defined by lapse rates; in the Troposphere, temperature decreases with altitude, while in the Stratosphere, it increases due to UV absorption by Ozone.
Insolation (Incoming Solar Radiation) is the solar energy intercepted by the earth; it decreases from the equator towards the poles, influencing global temperature belts.
Air pressure is highest at sea level and decreases with height; horizontally, the movement of air is governed by the pressure gradient and the Coriolis effect (implied in wind patterns).
The Thermosphere contains the Ionosphere (80-400 km), where electrically charged particles reflect low-frequency radio waves back to Earth.
Cyclonic circulation involves a low-pressure center surrounded by high-pressure winds; moisture condensation in this system releases latent heat, fueling the storm's intensity.
examples: Tropical Cyclones: The Odisha cyclone (October 1999) caused by high-velocity winds, tidal surges, and heavy rain, demonstrating atmospheric instability.
Global Wind Belts: The constant blowing of Trade Winds towards the Equator due to the subtropical high-pressure belt and equatorial low-pressure belt (Doldrums).
Orographic Rainfall: Clouds being forced to rise over a mountain range (e.g., Western Ghats), cooling and precipitating on the windward side.
Exosphere Composition: The uppermost layer where the air is extremely thin and light gases like Helium ($He$) and Hydrogen ($H_2$) float into space.
formulas: Relative Humidity: $\text{Actual Water Vapor Content} / \text{Max Capacity at that Temp} \times 100\%$
Wind Velocity: $V \propto \Delta P$ (Velocity is proportional to the pressure difference)
exam_tips: Analyze why the temperature in the Thermosphere rises so rapidly—it is due to the direct absorption of high-energy solar radiation.
Distinguish between 'Weather' (atmospheric state over short intervals) and 'Climate' (the statistical average over 30+ years).
Understand the concept of 'Condensation Nuclei': Water vapor needs dust or smoke particles to condense into droplets and form clouds.
Observe the relationship: Hot air is light and creates low pressure, while cold air is dense and creates high pressure.
